title: Aortic Regurgitation
oneLiner: Leaky aortic valve causing diastolic backflow into LV; chronic in bicuspid/rheumatic, acute in endocarditis/dissection; leads to LV volume overload and heart failure.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute severe AR (endocarditis / dissection):** shocked patient with wide pulse pressure, early diastolic murmur, acute pulmonary oedema — immediate echo needed; surgical emergency before ICU.
- **Aortic dissection (ascending):** tearing chest/back pain + pulse deficit + new AR murmur — single finding: **BP difference >20 mmHg between arms** with widened mediastinum on CXR.
- **Myocardial ischaemia mimicking AR:** chest pain + murmur — ECG and troponin to rule out STEMI before attributing murmur to chronic disease.
sections.Presentation:
- **Chronic:**
  - Slowly progressive dyspnoea on exertion
  - Palpitations (aware of heart beat)
  - Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance
  - Angina (coronary steal)
  - Often asymptomatic for years
- **Acute:**
  - Sudden severe dyspnoea and pulmonary oedema
  - Hypotension / shock
  - Fever with rigors (endocarditis)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Early diastolic murmur:** high-pitched, decrescendo, best heard at left sternal edge (3rd-4th ICS) with patient leaning forward, breath held after expiration
  - **Water-hammer pulse (Corrigan’s):** rapid upstroke and collapse of carotid pulse
  - **Wide pulse pressure** (elevated systolic, low diastolic)
  - **Austin Flint murmur:** presystolic rumble at apex (mimics mitral stenosis)
  - **Bounding pulses** (e.g. bisferiens, nail-bed capillary pulsations)
  - **Quincke’s sign:** pulsation of nail-bed capillaries with light pressure
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Aortic stenosis (AS):** systolic ejection murmur at right upper sternal border, narrow pulse pressure (displaced apex, slow rising carotid upstroke); splitting feature: **pulse character — water-hammer vs parvus et tardus**.
- **Mitral regurgitation (MR):** holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla; normal pulse; **obtain echo to differentiate**.
- **Ventricular septal defect:** holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border; often with thrill; **ECG and echo**.
- **Patent ductus arteriosus:** continuous machinery murmur, bounding pulses; **colour Doppler on echo**.
- **Pulmonary regurgitation:** Graham Steell murmur at left upper sternal border; usually not wide pulse pressure; **echo with right heart focus**.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Vital signs (BP both arms, HR, SpO₂, temperature)
  - ECG: may show LV hypertrophy (chronic), LV strain, or acute changes (if dissection/MI)
  - Urgent bedside echo (if available) — look for valve morphology, root, and LV function
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, CRP, troponin (rule out MI/myocarditis)
  - Blood cultures × 3 sets (if suspect endocarditis) — before antibiotics
  - BNP/NT-proBNP (if heart failure unclear)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) with Doppler — assess severity (vena contracta width ≥0.6 cm, regurgitant volume ≥60 mL, EROA ≥0.3 cm² for severe)
  - **Second-line (if TTE inadequate or suspect root pathology):** Transoesophageal echo (TOE) — especially acute AR, endocarditis, or dissection
  - **If aortic dissection suspected:** CT aortogram (or urgent TOE if unstable)
  - **Chest X-ray:** cardiomegaly (LV + dilated aorta), pulmonary oedema (acute)
- **Special tests:** Cardiac MRI (quantify LV volumes and regurgitation severity), exercise testing (if symptoms unclear)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Severity grading per ASE/EACVI guidelines:
    - Mild: vena contracta <0.3 cm, regurgitant fraction <30%
    - Moderate: 0.3-0.6 cm, 30-50%
    - Severe: >0.6 cm, >50% (or late reversal in descending aorta)
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Transthoracic echocardiogram** – delayed echo risks missing acute severe AR or root pathology; always do before assuming chronic stable disease.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Acute severe AR (shock / pulmonary oedema):**
    - Urgent cardiology + cardiac surgery consultation
    - Intra-aortic balloon pump often contraindicated (increases diastolic pressure worsens regurgitation) — avoid unless specific advice
    - Vasodilators (e.g. sodium nitroprusside 0.5–10 mcg/kg/min IV) to reduce afterload — [check local policy] for preparation and monitoring
    - Inotropes (e.g. dobutamine 2.5–20 mcg/kg/min IV) if systolic BP <90 mmHg — [check local policy]
    - Diuretics (e.g. furosemide 40–80 mg IV) for pulmonary oedema
  - **Pain / dissection:** manage per aortic dissection pathway (IV labetalol/esmolol to HR<60, plus vasodilator)
  - **Suspected endocarditis:** blood cultures then IV antibiotics per local protocol (e.g. benzylpenicillin + gentamicin or vancomycin) — [check local policy]
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Echo-confirmed chronic severe AR:
    - Optimise heart failure therapy: diuretics if fluid overload, ACE inhibitor (ramipril 2.5 mg od, uptitrate as tolerated) to reduce afterload
    - Vasodilator monotherapy (nifedipine slow-release 10-20 mg bd or ACE inhibitor) in asymptomatic patients with LV dilation
    - Serial troponin, ECG, BNP as needed
  - **Monitor pulse pressure, symptoms, urine output**
  - **Refer to cardiology for surgical timing**
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surgery indications for chronic severe AR:**
    - Symptomatic (dyspnoea, angina) despite medical therapy
    - LVEF <50%
    - LV end-systolic diameter >50 mm (or >55 mm if large body size)
    - LV end-diastolic diameter >65 mm
  - Aortic valve replacement (AVR) — mechanical vs bioprosthesis based on age/anticoagulation
  - Infective endocarditis: complete antibiotics ± valve surgery if vegetations >10 mm or abscess
  - Serial echo (every 6-12 months) for asymptomatic severe AR with normal LV function
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L/8h if hypotensive (titrate to BP) — avoid overload if heart failure
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if no bleeding risk; withhold if planned surgery)
  - Diet/NBM status: NBM if urgent surgery planned; otherwise normal diet
  - Observation frequency: 4-hourly vital signs (BP, HR, SpO₂, temp) — consider continuous ECG if acute
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **ACE inhibitor (chronic):** Ramipril 2.5 mg PO once daily, increase to 5-10 mg as tolerated (target BP <130/80). Monitor creatinine and K+ at initiation.
  - **Vasodilator (chronic):** Nifedipine MR 10–20 mg PO bd (alternative to ACE) — contraindicated if systolic <100 mmHg.
  - **Diuretic (acute fluid overload):** Furosemide 40-80 mg IV (repeated PRN) — max 120 mg in first 2h [check local policy].
  - **Inotrope (acute shock):** Dobutamine 2.5–20 mcg/kg/min IV — [check local policy] for infusion preparation.
  - **Vasodilator (acute severe AR):** Sodium nitroprusside 0.5–10 mcg/kg/min IV — **must be in ICU with arterial line**; protect from light; thiocyanate levels if >3 days.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Losartan 50–100 mg OD (if ACE intolerant)
  - Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate (if contraindication to ACE/ARB)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - ACE inhibitors: avoid in pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, hyperkalaemia, angio-oedema history
  - Nitroprusside: avoid in severe renal impairment (risk of cyanide toxicity)
  - Nifedipine: avoid in cardiogenic shock, caution with CYP3A4 inhibitors
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACE inhibitors: reduce dose if eGFR <30; avoid if eGFR <10 (unless on dialysis) — [check local policy]
  - Furosemide: dose adjustment in renal impairment (higher doses may be needed)
  - Nitroprusside: reduce dose in hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Nitroprusside infusion:** starting too fast causing profound hypotension and coronary hypoperfusion — **always use syringe pump, start at 0.5 mcg/kg/min, titrate every 5 min. Monitor arterial BP continuously.**
  - **Furosemide:** IV bolus given too fast (>4 mg/min) risks ototoxicity. Give over 1-2 minutes.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Pulmonary oedema / cardiogenic shock
  - Acute mitral regurgitation (due to LV dilation or annular distortion)
  - Aortic dissection (if root cause)
  - Infective endocarditis (with new AR murmur)
- **Chronic:**
  - LV eccentric hypertrophy → systolic dysfunction (LVEF <50%)
  - Heart failure (preserved then reduced ejection)
  - Arrhythmias (AF, ventricular tachycardias)
  - Sudden cardiac death (rare; occurs if severe LV dysfunction)
  - Endocarditis (even after valve replacement)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Chronic mild/moderate AR:** excellent, often asymptomatic for decades; survival similar to age-matched if LV function normal.
- **Chronic severe AR:** without surgery, 5-year survival ~50% if symptomatic; after AVR, 10-year survival 80-90% (worse if LVEF <50% pre-op).
- **Acute severe AR:** surgical mortality ~10-20%; without emergency surgery, mortality >90% from cardiogenic shock.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check BP in both arms** – >20 mmHg difference suggests aortic dissection with AR even if pain is absent.
- **Request urgent TTE in any patient with a new diastolic murmur + dyspnoea** – acute AR is a surgical emergency.
- **For chronic severe AR, measure LV end-systolic diameter yearly** – once >50 mm on echo, refer for surgery even if asymptomatic to prevent irreversible LV damage.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay surgery for acute severe AR** – waiting for "stabilisation" or overnight echo can be fatal; call cardiology and cardiac surgery immediately.
- **Do not give beta-blockers alone in acute AR** – bradycardia worsens regurgitation and can precipitate hypotension; only use if dissection with HR control (then combine with vasodilator).
- **Do not attribute wide pulse pressure + murmur to fever or anaemia** – always confirm with echo; missing chronic severe AR until LV failure occurs worsens outcome.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New shortness of breath or orthopnoea
  - Palpitations or syncope (even brief)
  - Fever >38°C with chills – may indicate endocarditis; urgent antibiotics and echo required
- **Follow-up:**
  - Cardiology outpatient within 2-4 weeks (sooner if severe AR and not yet surgical)
  - Repeat echocardiogram as per local guidelines (usually 6-12 months for asymptomatic severe AR)
  - Antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures no longer recommended routinely (NICE 2008 update) – only if previous infective endocarditis or valve prosthesis
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call cardiology registrar if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg or >180 mmHg
  - New oxygen requirement (SpO₂ <92% on air)
  - Chest pain with ECG changes (new ST/T wave)
- **Call cardiac surgery registrar if:**
  - Acute severe AR with shock or pulmonary oedema
  - Suspected aortic dissection (CT aortogram requested)
  - Endocarditis with >10 mm vegetation on echo
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Need for vasopressor/inotrope infusion
  - GCS drop <15 or seizure
  - Lactate >2.5 mmol/L and rising despite fluid resuscitation
  - Continuous ECG monitoring required

rawMarkdown:
# Aortic Regurgitation

**One-liner:** Leaky aortic valve causing diastolic backflow into LV; chronic in bicuspid/rheumatic, acute in endocarditis/dissection; leads to LV volume overload and heart failure.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute severe AR (endocarditis / dissection):** shocked patient with wide pulse pressure, early diastolic murmur, acute pulmonary oedema — immediate echo needed; surgical emergency before ICU.
- **Aortic dissection (ascending):** tearing chest/back pain + pulse deficit + new AR murmur — single finding: **BP difference >20 mmHg between arms** with widened mediastinum on CXR.
- **Myocardial ischaemia mimicking AR:** chest pain + murmur — ECG and troponin to rule out STEMI before attributing murmur to chronic disease.

### Presentation
- **Chronic:**
  - Slowly progressive dyspnoea on exertion
  - Palpitations (aware of heart beat)
  - Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance
  - Angina (coronary steal)
  - Often asymptomatic for years
- **Acute:**
  - Sudden severe dyspnoea and pulmonary oedema
  - Hypotension / shock
  - Fever with rigors (endocarditis)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Early diastolic murmur:** high-pitched, decrescendo, best heard at left sternal edge (3rd-4th ICS) with patient leaning forward, breath held after expiration
  - **Water-hammer pulse (Corrigan’s):** rapid upstroke and collapse of carotid pulse
  - **Wide pulse pressure** (elevated systolic, low diastolic)
  - **Austin Flint murmur:** presystolic rumble at apex (mimics mitral stenosis)
  - **Bounding pulses** (e.g. bisferiens, nail-bed capillary pulsations)
  - **Quincke’s sign:** pulsation of nail-bed capillaries with light pressure

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Aortic stenosis (AS):** systolic ejection murmur at right upper sternal border, narrow pulse pressure (displaced apex, slow rising carotid upstroke); splitting feature: **pulse character — water-hammer vs parvus et tardus**.
- **Mitral regurgitation (MR):** holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla; normal pulse; **obtain echo to differentiate**.
- **Ventricular septal defect:** holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border; often with thrill; **ECG and echo**.
- **Patent ductus arteriosus:** continuous machinery murmur, bounding pulses; **colour Doppler on echo**.
- **Pulmonary regurgitation:** Graham Steell murmur at left upper sternal border; usually not wide pulse pressure; **echo with right heart focus**.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Vital signs (BP both arms, HR, SpO₂, temperature)
  - ECG: may show LV hypertrophy (chronic), LV strain, or acute changes (if dissection/MI)
  - Urgent bedside echo (if available) — look for valve morphology, root, and LV function
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, CRP, troponin (rule out MI/myocarditis)
  - Blood cultures × 3 sets (if suspect endocarditis) — before antibiotics
  - BNP/NT-proBNP (if heart failure unclear)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) with Doppler — assess severity (vena contracta width ≥0.6 cm, regurgitant volume ≥60 mL, EROA ≥0.3 cm² for severe)
  - **Second-line (if TTE inadequate or suspect root pathology):** Transoesophageal echo (TOE) — especially acute AR, endocarditis, or dissection
  - **If aortic dissection suspected:** CT aortogram (or urgent TOE if unstable)
  - **Chest X-ray:** cardiomegaly (LV + dilated aorta), pulmonary oedema (acute)
- **Special tests:** Cardiac MRI (quantify LV volumes and regurgitation severity), exercise testing (if symptoms unclear)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Severity grading per ASE/EACVI guidelines:
    - Mild: vena contracta <0.3 cm, regurgitant fraction <30%
    - Moderate: 0.3-0.6 cm, 30-50%
    - Severe: >0.6 cm, >50% (or late reversal in descending aorta)
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Transthoracic echocardiogram** – delayed echo risks missing acute severe AR or root pathology; always do before assuming chronic stable disease.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Acute severe AR (shock / pulmonary oedema):**
    - Urgent cardiology + cardiac surgery consultation
    - Intra-aortic balloon pump often contraindicated (increases diastolic pressure worsens regurgitation) — avoid unless specific advice
    - Vasodilators (e.g. sodium nitroprusside 0.5–10 mcg/kg/min IV) to reduce afterload — [check local policy] for preparation and monitoring
    - Inotropes (e.g. dobutamine 2.5–20 mcg/kg/min IV) if systolic BP <90 mmHg — [check local policy]
    - Diuretics (e.g. furosemide 40–80 mg IV) for pulmonary oedema
  - **Pain / dissection:** manage per aortic dissection pathway (IV labetalol/esmolol to HR<60, plus vasodilator)
  - **Suspected endocarditis:** blood cultures then IV antibiotics per local protocol (e.g. benzylpenicillin + gentamicin or vancomycin) — [check local policy]
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Echo-confirmed chronic severe AR:
    - Optimise heart failure therapy: diuretics if fluid overload, ACE inhibitor (ramipril 2.5 mg od, uptitrate as tolerated) to reduce afterload
    - Vasodilator monotherapy (nifedipine slow-release 10-20 mg bd or ACE inhibitor) in asymptomatic patients with LV dilation
    - Serial troponin, ECG, BNP as needed
  - **Monitor pulse pressure, symptoms, urine output**
  - **Refer to cardiology for surgical timing**
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surgery indications for chronic severe AR:**
    - Symptomatic (dyspnoea, angina) despite medical therapy
    - LVEF <50%
    - LV end-systolic diameter >50 mm (or >55 mm if large body size)
    - LV end-diastolic diameter >65 mm
  - Aortic valve replacement (AVR) — mechanical vs bioprosthesis based on age/anticoagulation
  - Infective endocarditis: complete antibiotics ± valve surgery if vegetations >10 mm or abscess
  - Serial echo (every 6-12 months) for asymptomatic severe AR with normal LV function
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L/8h if hypotensive (titrate to BP) — avoid overload if heart failure
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if no bleeding risk; withhold if planned surgery)
  - Diet/NBM status: NBM if urgent surgery planned; otherwise normal diet
  - Observation frequency: 4-hourly vital signs (BP, HR, SpO₂, temp) — consider continuous ECG if acute

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **ACE inhibitor (chronic):** Ramipril 2.5 mg PO once daily, increase to 5-10 mg as tolerated (target BP <130/80). Monitor creatinine and K+ at initiation.
  - **Vasodilator (chronic):** Nifedipine MR 10–20 mg PO bd (alternative to ACE) — contraindicated if systolic <100 mmHg.
  - **Diuretic (acute fluid overload):** Furosemide 40-80 mg IV (repeated PRN) — max 120 mg in first 2h [check local policy].
  - **Inotrope (acute shock):** Dobutamine 2.5–20 mcg/kg/min IV — [check local policy] for infusion preparation.
  - **Vasodilator (acute severe AR):** Sodium nitroprusside 0.5–10 mcg/kg/min IV — **must be in ICU with arterial line**; protect from light; thiocyanate levels if >3 days.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Losartan 50–100 mg OD (if ACE intolerant)
  - Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate (if contraindication to ACE/ARB)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - ACE inhibitors: avoid in pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, hyperkalaemia, angio-oedema history
  - Nitroprusside: avoid in severe renal impairment (risk of cyanide toxicity)
  - Nifedipine: avoid in cardiogenic shock, caution with CYP3A4 inhibitors
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACE inhibitors: reduce dose if eGFR <30; avoid if eGFR <10 (unless on dialysis) — [check local policy]
  - Furosemide: dose adjustment in renal impairment (higher doses may be needed)
  - Nitroprusside: reduce dose in hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Nitroprusside infusion:** starting too fast causing profound hypotension and coronary hypoperfusion — **always use syringe pump, start at 0.5 mcg/kg/min, titrate every 5 min. Monitor arterial BP continuously.**
  - **Furosemide:** IV bolus given too fast (>4 mg/min) risks ototoxicity. Give over 1-2 minutes.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Pulmonary oedema / cardiogenic shock
  - Acute mitral regurgitation (due to LV dilation or annular distortion)
  - Aortic dissection (if root cause)
  - Infective endocarditis (with new AR murmur)
- **Chronic:**
  - LV eccentric hypertrophy → systolic dysfunction (LVEF <50%)
  - Heart failure (preserved then reduced ejection)
  - Arrhythmias (AF, ventricular tachycardias)
  - Sudden cardiac death (rare; occurs if severe LV dysfunction)
  - Endocarditis (even after valve replacement)

### Prognosis
- **Chronic mild/moderate AR:** excellent, often asymptomatic for decades; survival similar to age-matched if LV function normal.
- **Chronic severe AR:** without surgery, 5-year survival ~50% if symptomatic; after AVR, 10-year survival 80-90% (worse if LVEF <50% pre-op).
- **Acute severe AR:** surgical mortality ~10-20%; without emergency surgery, mortality >90% from cardiogenic shock.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check BP in both arms** – >20 mmHg difference suggests aortic dissection with AR even if pain is absent.
- **Request urgent TTE in any patient with a new diastolic murmur + dyspnoea** – acute AR is a surgical emergency.
- **For chronic severe AR, measure LV end-systolic diameter yearly** – once >50 mm on echo, refer for surgery even if asymptomatic to prevent irreversible LV damage.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay surgery for acute severe AR** – waiting for "stabilisation" or overnight echo can be fatal; call cardiology and cardiac surgery immediately.
- **Do not give beta-blockers alone in acute AR** – bradycardia worsens regurgitation and can precipitate hypotension; only use if dissection with HR control (then combine with vasodilator).
- **Do not attribute wide pulse pressure + murmur to fever or anaemia** – always confirm with echo; missing chronic severe AR until LV failure occurs worsens outcome.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New shortness of breath or orthopnoea
  - Palpitations or syncope (even brief)
  - Fever >38°C with chills – may indicate endocarditis; urgent antibiotics and echo required
- **Follow-up:**
  - Cardiology outpatient within 2-4 weeks (sooner if severe AR and not yet surgical)
  - Repeat echocardiogram as per local guidelines (usually 6-12 months for asymptomatic severe AR)
  - Antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures no longer recommended routinely (NICE 2008 update) – only if previous infective endocarditis or valve prosthesis

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call cardiology registrar if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg or >180 mmHg
  - New oxygen requirement (SpO₂ <92% on air)
  - Chest pain with ECG changes (new ST/T wave)
- **Call cardiac surgery registrar if:**
  - Acute severe AR with shock or pulmonary oedema
  - Suspected aortic dissection (CT aortogram requested)
  - Endocarditis with >10 mm vegetation on echo
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Need for vasopressor/inotrope infusion
  - GCS drop <15 or seizure
  - Lactate >2.5 mmol/L and rising despite fluid resuscitation
  - Continuous ECG monitoring required
